MySQL Service Stored Procedures

MySQL Service Stored Procedures

Service stored procedures are supported for the MySQL connector.

The stored procedure name used for execution by Uniface is derived from the component name and the operation name:

The stored procedure name used for execution by Uniface is depends on the whether literal names have been defined in the signature of the Stored Procedure Component.

Table 1. Component Name and Operation Name Specification
Literal Component Name Literal Operation Name Stored Procedure Name Example
— — UnifaceComponentName.UnifaceOperationName

MY_CPT.SP1

— Specified LiteralOperationName 1

sp1

SchemaName.OperationName 2

schema.sp1

Specified — LiteralComponentName.UnifaceOperationName

sp.SP1

Specified Specified LiteralComponentName.LiteralOperationName

sp.sp1

SchemaName.OperationName 2

schema.sp1

  • UnifaceComponentName: MY_CPT
  • UnifaceOperationName: SP1
  1. If LiteralOperationName does not include a schema name, the default schema of the user is used.
  2. If LiteralOperationName includes a schema name (for example, SchemaName.OperationName), the stored procedure will be called as SchemaName.OperationName. If a literal component name is also specified, it is ignored.
CAUTION: This is not recommended because it will not run on other databases connectors.

Parameter names are not used during the execution of stored procedures. However, their order (that is, their relative position) is used for mapping parameters. For more information, see Naming Specifications for Service Store Procedures.

Entity Parameters

Because of a limitation in the current MySQL release, ‘prepared statements’ do not support multiple result sets. Therefore, the current Uniface MySQL connector does not support stored procedures with multiple entity parameters—a maximum of one entity parameter is allowed. For more information about Prepared Statement Problems, refer https://dev.mysql.com/.

Furthermore, when defining an operation with an entity parameter in a Uniface signature, you can define an entity parameter only as OUT or INOUT, but when creating the corresponding stored procedure in MySQL, you must define the parameter as an INPUT string. Also, the name of the Uniface entity parameter must be identical to the name of the table in the database. See the example below.

Data Conversions

The data types of operation parameters must closely match the stored procedure parameters. If necessary, explicit and implicit conversions are used to achieve an optimal mapping for all data types.

The supported parameter data type mappings for MySQL are shown in the following table. (The data type mappings shown in the table apply to both basic parameters and entity parameters.)

Table 3. Uniface to MySQL parameter data type mappings
MySQL data type String Boolean Numeric Float (L)Date (L)Time (L)Datetime
char Y Y Y Y
varchar Y Y Y Y
decimal Y Y Y Y
tinyint Y Y Y Y
smallint Y Y Y Y
mediumint Y Y Y Y
bigint Y Y Y Y
int Y Y Y Y
float Y Y Y
date Y Y Y Y
datetime Y Y Y Y
time Y Y Y

IN, OUT, and INOUT Parameters

All parameter data is passed to MySQL as bound data. To achieve optimal mapping for all parameters, MySQL parameter data type and operator parameter data type information is used to determine the MySQL binding data types.

NULL Support

Empty input string values are mapped to MySQL values as NULL values. There is one exception to this: empty Uniface boolean data types are always mapped to FALSE. This is standard functionality.

Exception Behavior

The exceptions shown in the following table are detected by the MySQL connector:

Table 4. Exception message numbers and meanings
$procerror Meaning
-21 Connection failure
-55 Input parameters failure
-56 Output parameter failure
-150 Connector or MySQL failure
-166 Operation is not stateless
-1406 Memory allocation failure

The following examples illustrate the use of Service Stored Procedures.

The first example shows a Service Stored Procedure with two basic parameters. It is executed with the following activate ProcScript statement:

activate "WORKTODO".STR_TEST($in$, $out$)

The STR_TEST operation has the parameters shown in the table:

Parameter name Type Data type Input/Output
P1 Basic String Input
P2 Basic String Output

The MySQL script is as follows:

DELIMITER $$
DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS 'WORKTODO'.'STR_TEST' $$
CREATE PROCEDURE 'WORKTODO'.'STR_TEST' (IN P1 VARCHAR(40), OUT P2 VARCHAR(40))
BEGIN
SET P2 = P1;
END $$
DELIMITER ;

The second example shows a Service Stored Procedure with one entity parameter. It is executed with the following activate ProcScript statement:

activate "WORKTODO".ENT_TEST("mytable.mymodel")

The ENT_TEST operation has the single output entity parameter shown in the table:

Parameter Name Type Data Type Input/Output
MYTABLE Entity — Output
Table 5. MYTABLE Entity Fields
Field Name Field Type Data Type
STR S VC40
NUM N C20

The MySQL script is as follows:

DELIMITER $$
DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS 'WORKTODO'.'ENT_TEST' $$
CREATE PROCEDURE 'WORKTODO'.'ENT_TEST' (IN mytable(40))
BEGIN
SELECT * from mytable;
END $$
DELIMITER ;

(Note the line with the declaration IN mytable).
